title: 29 Tiqimt — The Triple Feast & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "29 Tiqimt — The Triple Feast & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"29 Tiqimt — The Triple Feast & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Tiqimt

The Triple Feast: Maskal, Qal and Timqat (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Anastasia the Roman, Martyr

St. Abraham of Rostov, Monastic Founder

The twenty-ninth day of Tiqimt carries the monthly Triple Feast — the simultaneous commemoration of Maskal, Qal, and Timqat — and in the month of interiority this recapitulation of the three great mysteries is received differently than it was in Meskerem's golden opening. The Cross is contemplated not as a Demera bonfire blazing on the highland hilltop but as the interior axis of the daily life — the daily Cross that the month has been teaching. The Word is contemplated not as the prophetic proclamation that echoes across the mountains but as the still, small voice that speaks in the cell and the night and the desert cave. The Baptism is contemplated not as the great Timqat procession at the river but as the daily renewal of the baptismal dying and rising that the interior life practices in its continuous, unspectacular way. In Tiqimt, the Triple Feast arrives in its interior form, and the community that has been formed by the month is now capable of receiving it more completely than it could in Meskerem's first freshness.

St. Anastasia the Roman — a young woman martyr whose story has the quality of the great female martyrdom narratives of the early Church — brings the blood of the young and the strong into the penultimate day's assembly. She refused apostasy with the completeness of the young who have not yet learned to negotiate with what they believe, and her witness in Tiqimt's context is the testimony that the interior life formed in youth — if it is genuinely formed — can produce a clarity of response in extremity that is not available to the untested. The young martyr who dies without hesitation has not failed to count the cost; she has counted it completely and found it unequivocal. In the month of interiority, Anastasia the Roman is the gift of the completely formed young conscience, the witness that the covenant of mercy is not only for those who have lived long enough to understand its depths.

St. Abraham of Rostov was a twelfth-century Russian monastic who, tradition holds, destroyed a pagan idol in the region of Rostov-on-the-Lake and converted the population of the area by a combination of holy living and miraculous power. He founded a monastery on the site of the idol's destruction — the monastery of the Theophany — and is considered the apostle of the Rostov region. His story connects the interior life of the monastic to the missionary act: the solitary who founded a community on the ground where the false god had stood, the contemplative whose cell became the center of a people's conversion. In the penultimate day of the month of mercy, Abraham of Rostov is the testimony that the interior life's fruit is always, finally, a new beginning — that the month of interiority does not end in itself but opens into the apostolic mission that the next month will commission.

The Triple Feast, Anastasia the Roman, and Abraham of Rostov together on the twenty-ninth day of Tiqimt compose the month's penultimate movement: the triple mystery in its interior form, the young martyr's unqualified yes, and the monastic apostle whose cell became a mission. One day remains. The community that has been formed by Tiqimt's interior life — its quietness, its steadfastness, its sustained attention to the covenant of mercy — stands on the eve of the month's final day with everything it has received, preparing to carry it into the world that the thirtieth day will open onto.
